Implement the permanent, root-cause fixes (no band-aids ‚Äî the owner rejects anything temporary) for these four bugs, all in packages/ai:
1. OpenAI strict structured output rejects the extraction schema because optional Zod fields are absent from the JSON-schema `required` array. In packages/ai/src/providers/openai-adapter.ts the helper stripIncompatibleFields()/walk() recomputes required=all keys. Make it PERFECT: for each property that was NOT originally required, also make it nullable (union its type with "null", handling type-as-string, type-as-array, anyOf/oneOf/$ref). Keep this transform OpenAI-ONLY (do not change the schema given to Gemini/Vertex adapters). Optional fields are at roughly lines 152,190,201,205,210,223 of extraction-system.ts.
2. Gemini Google-API hard 60s timeout ‚Äî already replaced with DEFAULT_GEMINI_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=180_000 + GOOGLE_GEMINI_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS env + constructor option in google-gemini-adapter.ts. Verify no hard-coded 60_000/60000 remains in ANY adapter and that the timeout applies on BOTH the API-key SDK path and the service-account OAuth/REST path.
3. Gemini thinkingLevel breaks gemini-2.5-flash vision. Current guard `if (!route.supportsReasoningControls) return {}` in geminiThinkingConfig() is INSUFFICIENT because packages/ai/src/routes/catalog.ts:49 marks gemini-2.5-flash supportsReasoningControls:true. Fix properly via the capability layer (catalog.ts / candidates.ts / routes/types.ts): model facts must encode how/whether each model expresses thinking per-modality, with NO hard-coded model-id checks in adapter logic. The likely correct shape: thinking config must not be sent on vision (image-input) calls and/or gemini-2.5-flash uses thinkingBudget not the thinkingLevel enum ‚Äî encode this in capability metadata and have the adapter honor it. Since you cannot hit the live API here, implement the most-correct capability-driven design and clearly document the exact live-API assertion that must be run locally to confirm.
4. Settings value double-JSON-encoded. Trace the writer: apps/web/app/admin/settings/_components/model-picker.tsx POSTs (lines ~267,280) to an API route ‚Üí the `settings` table (Drizzle schema imported in apps/web/app/admin/settings/page.tsx). Find the exact line that JSON.stringify's an already-encoded value and make writes idempotent; ensure writer, reader (page.tsx loader), and the router's settings reader all agree on one canonical encoding. Add a regression unit/verify test that fails on double-encoding.

Run gates that don't need network: pnpm -w typecheck, pnpm -w lint, pnpm -w build (record a baseline first; introduce no new failures; no @ts-expect-error or .catch() workarounds). Add/extend tests under packages/ai/src/__verify__.

Commit your changes to a NEW branch named fix/adapter-bugs-permanent (NOT main) with a conventional-commit message + `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>` trailer, and push the branch so it can be reviewed and the live/deploy/bake-off steps finished locally. Do NOT touch production settings, do NOT deploy, do NOT commit to main, never commit secrets.

FINAL REPORT (your returned message): the STEP 0 probe results; per-bug what you changed (file:line) and why it is the permanent fix; gate results vs baseline; the branch name + commit SHA pushed; and a precise, ordered checklist of exactly what remains to be done locally with live credentials (live-API verification per model, worker deploy to Trigger project proj_wgpzsvhmsopqhvwqaycn, the full bake-off per fix_TESTPLAN.md, and setting prod to the winner). 1	# fix_ADAPTER_BUGS.md ‚Äî Permanent, root-cause fixes for the four model-adapter bugs
2	
3	**Audience:** a fresh AI engineering session with no memory of how these bugs were found.
4	**Mandate from the repo owner (Albert):** *"Proper, long-term fix. Never a short-term fix or band-aid. Never accept anything less than perfect, not for now, not ever."* Treat every instruction here as a floor, not a ceiling. If you find a deeper or more correct fix than what is written, take it ‚Äî but never a narrower or more temporary one.
5	
6	You must carry this all the way to completion **without asking the owner anything**: fix ‚Üí prove against live provider APIs ‚Üí deploy the worker ‚Üí re-run the full extraction/vision bake-off ‚Üí set production to the genuinely-best models ‚Üí commit + push ‚Üí write the results report. Everything you need is below or discoverable in the repo. Do not stop at "code compiles." Done means "proven correct against the real APIs in production and the bake-off re-run."
7	
8	---
9	
10	## 0. Context: what these bugs are and how they were discovered
11	
12	The Oracle ingests images and documents in two passes:
13	1. **VISION** ‚Äî a vision model transcribes an uploaded image to text (`default_vision_route`).
14	2. **EXTRACTION** ‚Äî a text model turns that transcript into claims JSON (`default_extraction_route`).
15	
16	Inference path: `OracleAIClient ‚Üí ModelRouter ‚Üí provider adapter`. Approved models live in DB settings pools (`model_pool_extraction`, etc.). When a model fails, the pool advances to the next candidate (`AllCandidatesFailedError` only if *all* fail). **This pool-advancement masked the bugs:** a model that 400s instantly looks "fine" because the chain silently moves on to one that works.
17	
18	A controlled bake-off (isolating each model into a one-element pool) revealed that **3 of 4 extraction models and 1 of 2 vision models are hard-broken in current production.** The only models that actually run today are extraction=`gemini-3.1-flash-lite` (works but under-extracts ‚Äî ~7 claims from a ~74-edge diagram) and vision=`qwen3-vl` (verified accurate). Everything else fails before producing a claim. The four root causes are below.
19	
20	The owner does **not** want the weak-but-working `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` as the permanent extractor. He wants every candidate model genuinely working so the bake-off can pick the best on quality, not on "it was the only one that didn't crash."
21	
22	**Partial fixes already exist in the working tree** (started by a prior session). Do not assume they are correct or complete ‚Äî verify each against the acceptance criteria and the live API. Some are wrong (see Bug 3).
23	
24	---
25	
26	## 1. THE FOUR BUGS
27	
28	### Bug 1 ‚Äî OpenAI strict structured output rejects the extraction schema
29	**Symptom:** `gpt-4o-mini` and `gpt-4.1-mini` extraction calls 400 instantly with an error about a missing property (e.g. `Missing 'sensitivityReason'`). Both OpenAI extraction models are therefore unusable.
30	
31	**Root cause:** OpenAI's strict JSON-schema mode (`response_format.json_schema.strict: true`) requires that **every** property appear in the object's `required` array (and `additionalProperties: false`). The Zod‚ÜíJSON-schema converter only lists non-`.optional()` keys in `required`. The extraction schema has several optional fields (`packages/ai/src/prompts/extraction-system.ts` ‚Äî `.optional()` at roughly lines 152, 190, 201, 205, 210, 223, including `sensitivityReason` / `sensitivityFlags`). Those optional keys are absent from `required`, so strict mode 400s.
32	
33	**Current partial fix (verify, then make perfect):** `packages/ai/src/providers/openai-adapter.ts` has `stripIncompatibleFields()` ‚Üí `walk()` (~line 387) that deletes `$schema`/`default` and recomputes `required = Object.keys(properties)` for every object node. It is applied in both `generateObject` (line ~110) and `submitBatch` (line ~174).
34	
35	**Why this is not yet "perfect" and what you must do:**
36	- Forcing a genuinely-optional field into `required` without also making it **nullable** forces the model to always emit a value, which is semantically wrong for fields that are meant to be absent sometimes. The canonical OpenAI-strict pattern is: *every property is in `required`, and formerly-optional properties become nullable* (`type: ["string","null"]`, or a union with `{"type":"null"}`). Implement that in `walk()`: for each property key that was **not** originally in the object's `required` set, union its type with `null` (handle `type` as string, `type` as array, and `anyOf`/`oneOf`/`$ref` shapes). Then recompute `required` to all keys.
37	- Confirm the downstream Zod parse still accepts a `null` for those fields (the schema uses `.optional()`; `null` is not `undefined`). If Zod rejects `null`, either map `.optional()` ‚Üí `.nullish()` in the schema, or normalize `null`‚Üí`undefined` before validation in the OpenAI adapter. Pick whichever keeps a single source of truth and does not weaken validation for the other providers. **Do not** silently coerce or `.catch()` away validation errors (a prior session tried that; it was reverted as a band-aid).
38	- The strip/nullable transform must be **OpenAI-only**. Gemini/Vertex must keep receiving the original schema (they do not require all-keys-required and they reject some OpenAI-isms). Verify you are not changing the shared `zodToJsonSchema` output for the other adapters.
39	
40	**Acceptance:** `gpt-4o-mini` AND `gpt-4.1-mini` complete a real extraction call against the live OpenAI API on the bake-off document and return schema-valid claims (no 400, no refusal). Optional fields that the model legitimately omits come back as `null`/absent and pass Zod.
41	
42	---
43	
44	### Bug 2 ‚Äî Gemini (Google Developer API) hard 60-second timeout
45	**Symptom:** `google/gemini-2.5-flash` extraction aborts at ~60.1s on dense image-derived transcripts, every time.
46	
47	**Root cause:** a hard-coded `AbortSignal.timeout(60_000)` in the Google Gemini adapter.
48	
49	**Current partial fix (verify):** `packages/ai/src/providers/google-gemini-adapter.ts` now defines `DEFAULT_GEMINI_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS = 180_000`, a `requestTimeoutMs` constructor option, and a `GOOGLE_GEMINI_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS` env override; the SDK client gets `httpOptions: { timeout: this.requestTimeoutMs }` and the OAuth/REST path uses `AbortSignal.timeout(this.requestTimeoutMs)`.
50	
51	**What you must do:** Verify there is **no remaining hard-coded 60s** anywhere in this adapter or any other adapter (grep `60_000`, `60000`, `timeout`). Confirm the timeout applies on **both** code paths (API-key SDK path and service-account OAuth/REST path). Confirm 180s is comfortably above the real p99 for a dense extraction (the bake-off transcripts can take >60s; if you observe calls approaching 180s, raise the default and note it). A timeout must never be the thing that kills a legitimate call.
52	
53	**Acceptance:** `google/gemini-2.5-flash` completes a real extraction on the bake-off document via the Google Developer API without an abort, on whichever auth path production actually uses.
54	
55	---
56	
57	### Bug 3 ‚Äî Gemini `thinkingLevel` unconditionally attached, breaks gemini-2.5-flash vision (NOT actually fixed yet)
58	**Symptom:** `gemini-2.5-flash` as a **vision** model 400s with "Thinking level not supported" (or equivalent).
59	
60	**Root cause:** `geminiThinkingConfig()` attached a `thinkingConfig.thinkingLevel` to every call.
61	
62	**Current partial fix is INSUFFICIENT ‚Äî this is the one that is still broken.** `geminiThinkingConfig(route)` now early-returns `{}` when `!route.supportsReasoningControls`. **But the model catalog marks `gemini-2.5-flash` with `supportsReasoningControls: true`** (`packages/ai/src/routes/catalog.ts:49`). So for `gemini-2.5-flash` the guard does nothing ‚Äî `thinkingLevel` is still attached and vision still 400s. The partial fix only helps models already flagged `false`.
63	
64	**You must determine the true root cause against the live API and fix it properly.** Investigate and decide among (not limited to) these ‚Äî verify empirically, do not guess:
65	- Whether `gemini-2.5-flash` rejects the **`thinkingLevel` enum** specifically but accepts a numeric **`thinkingBudget`** (the older field). If so, the adapter should emit the field shape the target model actually supports ‚Äî i.e. capability must encode *how* a model expresses thinking, not just a boolean.
66	- Whether thinking config is simply invalid when the request includes **image input** (vision) for this model, regardless of text support ‚Äî in which case vision calls must never carry thinking config.
67	- Whether the catalog flag for `gemini-2.5-flash` is itself wrong for the vision use, requiring a more precise capability model (e.g. `thinking` supported for text but the `thinkingLevel` enum unsupported on this model generation).
68	
69	The "perfect" fix is a capability model that is correct per-model and per-modality, driven from `catalog.ts`/`candidates.ts` metadata ‚Äî **not** a special-case `if (modelId === 'gemini-2.5-flash')`. No hard-coded model names in adapter logic (the owner has been explicit and repeatedly insistent: **nothing model-specific may be hard-coded in adapters**; model facts live in the catalog/capability layer and flow through the route).
70	
71	**Acceptance:** `gemini-2.5-flash` completes a real **vision** transcription of the bake-off image via the live API with no thinking-related 400, while any model that *does* support `thinkingLevel` still receives it for reasoning-bearing roles. Re-verify Bug 2's `google/gemini-2.5-flash` extraction still passes after this change.
72	
73	---
74	
75	### Bug 4 ‚Äî Settings value double-JSON-encoded (data fixed; find & fix the WRITER)
76	**Symptom:** `default_extraction_reasoning_effort` was stored as a double-encoded string (`"\"off\""` / `"\"google/gemini-2.5-flash\""`-style) in the `settings` table, so consumers read a quoted string instead of the value. The **data** has already been repaired to a single-encoded `"off"` in production. **The code path that produced it has NOT been fixed.**
77	
78	**Root cause to find:** trace the write path from `apps/web/app/admin/settings/_components/model-picker.tsx` (it `fetch`/POSTs a JSON body at ~lines 267, 280) ‚Üí the API route handler it calls ‚Üí the `settings` table write (Drizzle `settings` schema, imported in `apps/web/app/admin/settings/page.tsx`). Somewhere a value that is already a JSON string is `JSON.stringify`'d again (double-encode), or a value that should be stored raw is being stringified when the column/consumer expects the bare value. Find the exact line and fix it so a write is **idempotent**: writing `off` stores `off` (or a single, consistent canonical encoding), and reading it back yields `off`. Whatever canonical form you choose (raw text vs. single-JSON), make the **writer, the reader, and every other settings consumer agree** ‚Äî verify there is not a second writer (e.g. the bake-off `_set` tooling, server actions, or migrations) using a different convention.
79	
80	**Acceptance:** Round-trip test: set each model/effort setting through the **real admin write path**, read it back through the **real consumer path** (`page.tsx` loader + the router's settings reader), and confirm no extra quoting at any layer. Add a regression guard (unit test or a `__verify__` assertion) that fails if a settings value is double-encoded.
81	
82	---
83	
84	## 2. PROVE IT ‚Äî no fix counts until it runs against the live APIs
85

33	
34	export const EXTRACTION_PROMPT_VERSION = '2.2.0';
35	
36	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
37	// Claim type taxonomy (spec 9.4 + Part 6).
38	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
39	export const CLAIM_TYPES = [
40	  'process_rule',      // How something normally works
41	  'exception_rule',    // An exception to a standard process
42	  'bottleneck',        // A constraint or delay point (person, system, or approval)
43	  'workaround',        // An unofficial solution for a missing capability
44	  'system_limitation', // What a system (Coldlion, ERP, etc.) fails to do
45	  'dependency',        // Something that must occur before something else
46	  'handoff_gap',       // Information lost between departments
47	  'contradiction',     // Two conflicting facts about the same process
48	  'process_ambiguity', // Unclear ownership or unclear when/how something happens
49	] as const;
50	
51	export type ClaimType = (typeof CLAIM_TYPES)[number];
52	
53	// Group chat semantic roles (spec 9.5).
54	export const SEMANTIC_ROLES = [
55	  'claim_stated',
56	  'claim_confirmed',
57	  'claim_challenged',
58	  'claim_refined',
59	  'exception_introduced',
60	  'process_ambiguity_revealed',
61	] as const;
62	
63	export type SemanticRole = (typeof SEMANTIC_ROLES)[number];
64	
65	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
66	// Zod output schema for generateObject calls.
67	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
68	
69	export const ExtractionEvidenceSchema = z.object({
70	  exactQuote: z
71	    .string()
72	    .min(5)
73	    .describe(
74	      'Verbatim substring from the source message ‚Äî must appear character-for-character in the message content. Do not paraphrase.',
75	    ),
76	  sourceMessageId: z
77	    .string()
78	    .describe(
79	      'The ID of the source this quote was taken from. For chat extraction, this must be one of the provided message IDs. For document extraction, this must be one of the provided document chunk IDs.',
80	    ),
81	  confidence: z
82	    .number()
83	    .int()
84	    .min(1)
85	    .max(10)
86	    .describe('Confidence that this quote supports the claim (1=uncertain, 10=certain).'),
87	});
88	
89	export const ExtractionGapSchema = z.object({
90	  questionToAsk: z
91	    .string()
92	    .min(10)
93	    .describe('A follow-up question that would resolve an uncertainty revealed in this segment.'),
94	  whyItMatters: z
95	    .string()
96	    .min(10)
97	    .describe('Why resolving this gap is operationally important for POP Creations / Spruce Line.'),
98	  priority: z.enum(['low', 'medium', 'high', 'urgent']),
99	});
100	
101	/**
102	 * R5.5 ‚Äî Entity reference the model surfaced from the text. The validator
103	 * resolves these against the canonical entity registry; the licensor-vs-
104	 * vendor type-mismatch check fires here if the model proposes a known
105	 * licensor under `entity_type = 'vendor'`.
106	 */
107	export const ExtractionEntityProposalSchema = z.object({
108	  entityType: z
109	    .enum(ENTITY_TYPES as unknown as [string, ...string[]])
110	    .describe(
111	      'The kind of entity. licensor is for entertainment-IP rights holders (Disney, Marvel, Star Wars, Warner Bros, NBCUniversal) ‚Äî NEVER use vendor for those. vendor is the residual bucket for non-customer non-factory non-licensor business partners.',
112	    ),

114	    .string()
115	    .min(1)

119	    ),
120	});
121	
122	/**
123	 * R5.5 / P1 #2 ‚Äî Sensitivity flags. STRICT mode: only flag content that is
124	 * clearly disciplinary, medical, compensation, or hostile interpersonal.
125	 * Operational mentions of named employees are NOT sensitive ‚Äî e.g. "Jordan
126	 * handles licensing" is fine. The flag only fires when the content itself
127	 * describes personnel/HR/medical/conflict material that shouldn't live in
128	 * the operational knowledge graph.
129	 *
130	 * Setting any of these true causes the candidate to be quarantined and

132	 */
133	export const ExtractionSensitivityFlagsSchema = z.object({
134	  containsSensitiveHRData: z
135	    .boolean()
136	    .describe(
137	      'True ONLY if the message contains: formal disciplinary action (warning, PIP, termination), compensation specifics (salary numbers, bonus amounts, raise discussions), formal performance reviews/ratings, or formally documented LOA reasons (medical leave, family leave). NOT true for casual operational mentions of who is on vacation, who handles a process, or who is out.',
138	    ),
139	  containsSensitivePersonalData: z
140	    .boolean()
141	    .describe(
142	      'True ONLY if the message contains explicit personal information: medical conditions or diagnoses, family situations (divorce, deaths, dependents), legal issues (lawsuits, arrests, immigration status), home addresses or personal contact details, or other clearly private personal facts unrelated to operational work.',
143	    ),
144	  isPersonalConflict: z
145	    .boolean()
146	    .describe(
147	      'True ONLY if the message describes explicit interpersonal hostility BETWEEN NAMED INDIVIDUALS: shouting matches, harassment accusations, character attacks, or formal complaints filed against another employee. NOT true for normal disagreement about a process or generic team frustration.',
148	    ),
149	  sensitivityReason: z
150	    .string()
151	    .max(500)
152	    .nullish()
153	    .describe(
154	      'If any of the three flags is true, briefly explain which specific spans triggered the flag. Omit if all three are false.',
155	    ),
156	});
157	
158	export const ExtractionClaimSchema = z.object({
159	  claimType: z.enum(CLAIM_TYPES),
160	  summary: z
161	    .string()
162	    .min(10)
163	    .max(1000)
164	    .describe(
165	      'One declarative sentence stating what this claim asserts about company operations. Should be independently comprehensible without the source messages.',
166	    ),
167	  impactScore: z
168	    .number()
169	    .int()
170	    .min(1)
171	    .max(10)
172	    .describe('Business impact if this claim is true (1=trivial curiosity, 10=critical blocker).'),
173	  confidenceScore: z
174	    .number()
175	    .int()
176	    .min(1)
177	    .max(10)
178	    .describe(
179	      'How confident you are that this is a reliable, well-supported operational claim (1=speculative, 10=certain from explicit statement).',
180	    ),
181	  domains: z
182	    .array(z.enum(KNOWLEDGE_DOMAINS as unknown as [string, ...string[]]))
183	    .min(1)
184	    .describe(
185	      'Which knowledge domains this claim belongs to (at least one required). Use general for cross-functional, end-to-end, or whole-company process-flow claims that explain how work moves across departments.',
186	    ),
187	  evidence: ExtractionEvidenceSchema,
188	  semanticRole: z
189	    .enum(SEMANTIC_ROLES)
190	    .nullish()
191	    .describe(
192	      'In group chats: how this claim was introduced. Omit for single-speaker segments.',
193	    ),
194	  requiresReview: z
195	    .boolean()
196	    .describe(
197	      'Set true if any of: impact >= 7, the claim names a specific person as a bottleneck, or the claim implies customer or licensor risk.',
198	    ),
199	  proposedEntities: z
200	    .array(ExtractionEntityProposalSchema)
201	    .nullish()
202	    .describe(
203	      'Distinct entities (customers, licensors, factories, systems, departments, geographies, etc.) referenced in this claim. Omit if no proper-noun entities are mentioned.',
204	    ),
205	  sensitivityFlags: ExtractionSensitivityFlagsSchema.nullish().describe(
206	    'Set when any of the three flags is true. Omit when nothing is sensitive (the common case for operational claims).',
207	  ),
208	  suggestedGaps: z
209	    .array(ExtractionGapSchema)
210	    .nullish()
211	    .describe(
212	      'Follow-up questions that would resolve uncertainties or ambiguities revealed in this segment.',
213	    ),
214	});

216	export const ExtractionOutputSchema = z.object({
217	  claims: z
218	    .array(ExtractionClaimSchema)
219	    .describe('All operational claims extracted from this conversation segment. Empty array if no claims found.'),
220	  segmentSummary: z
221	    .string()
222	    .max(2000)
223	    .nullish()
224	    .describe(
225	      'Brief (1‚Äì2 sentence) summary of what this conversation segment covered operationally. Keep it concise ‚Äî well under 2000 characters. Omit if the segment was trivial or off-topic.',
226	    ),

229	export type ExtractionOutput = z.infer<typeof ExtractionOutputSchema>;
230	export type ExtractionClaim = z.infer<typeof ExtractionClaimSchema>;

234	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
235	// System prompt text.
236	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
237	
238	export const EXTRACTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT = `You are an operational knowledge extractor for The Oracle.
239	
240	The Oracle is an AI knowledge graph mapping the "dark matter" of POP Creations / Spruce Line ‚Äî a high-volume home decor company (Brooklyn, NY). They design in-house, manufacture in China, and sell to Burlington, TJX, Ross, Hobby Lobby, Walmart, and others. They use an ERP system called Coldlion. They license from Disney, Marvel, Star Wars / Lucasfilm, Warner Bros, and NBCUniversal.
241	
242	YOUR TASK: Analyze the provided conversation segment and extract concrete, evidence-backed operational claims about how this company actually works. For each claim, also surface:
243	  (1) the entities it references (customers, licensors, factories, systems, departments, geographies),
244	  (2) whether the source content is sensitive in a way that should keep the claim OUT of the operational knowledge graph.
245	
246	CLAIM TYPES:
247	- process_rule: How something normally works ("We always send artwork to China after licensor approval")
248	- exception_rule: An exception to a standard process ("For Walmart seasonal, sourcing sees it earlier")
249	- bottleneck: A constraint, delay, or dependency on a specific person/system/approval
250	- workaround: An unofficial solution someone built because the official system is insufficient
251	- system_limitation: What a system (Coldlion, ERP, email, spreadsheet) fails to do or show
252	- dependency: Something that must occur before something else can proceed
253	- handoff_gap: Information that tends to get lost between departments or people
254	- contradiction: Two conflicting statements about the same process
255	- process_ambiguity: Unclear ownership, unclear when something happens, or unclear who is responsible
256	
257	GROUP CHAT SEMANTICS ‚Äî track how claims interact when multiple employees speak:
258	- claim_stated: First assertion of a new fact
259	- claim_confirmed: Another employee agrees
260	- claim_challenged: Another employee disagrees
261	- claim_refined: A nuance or caveat is added to a prior claim
262	- exception_introduced: An exception to a stated rule is revealed
263	- process_ambiguity_revealed: The conversation reveals that employees have conflicting understandings
264	
265	EXTRACTION RULES:
266	1. Only extract claims supported by what employees actually said.
267	2. exactQuote MUST be a verbatim substring of the source message ‚Äî never paraphrase.
268	3. sourceMessageId must be one of the source IDs provided ‚Äî for chat, use a message ID; for document ingestion, use a document chunk ID. Do not invent IDs.
269	4. Do NOT extract: pleasantries, greetings, Oracle assistant messages, or obvious generic facts with no operational specificity.
270	5. A single message may yield 0, 1, or multiple claims.
271	6. Set requiresReview=true if: impact >= 7, the claim names a specific person as a bottleneck, OR the claim implies customer or licensor risk.
272	7. Suggest gaps (follow-up questions) when the segment reveals uncertainty or ambiguity that needs resolution.
273	8. Do not flatten group conversation ‚Äî if two employees express different views about the same process, extract both as separate claims with appropriate semantic roles and potentially a contradiction.
274	9. Classify claims by their operational meaning, not by whether a department name or keyword literally appears. A document can describe Licensing work without the word "licensing"; infer the relevant domain from responsibilities, approvals, handoffs, systems, and decisions described in the text.
275	10. Use the \`general\` domain for end-to-end business-process or companywide workflow claims that explain how work moves across multiple departments. Also include narrower domains such as \`licensing\`, \`design\`, \`production\`, \`sourcing\`, \`logistics\`, \`customers\`, \`sales\`, \`costing\`, or \`coldlion\` when the same claim is materially about those areas. \`costing\` means product/SKU costing and customer product pricing, not company finance/accounting. A costing sheet created by Design and sent to factories should usually include \`costing\` plus \`design\`, \`production\`, and/or \`sourcing\` if those handoffs are part of the claim.
276	11. Prefer small, reviewable operational claims over broad document summaries. Do not turn an entire form, spreadsheet, or SOP into one vague claim. Extract the concrete rule, handoff, responsibility, input, output, or exception.
277	12. For handoff claims, include every materially involved workflow domain. Example: "Design sends the costing sheet to factories so factories can quote production costs" is not just \`costing\`; it also involves \`design\` and \`sourcing\`/factory communication.
278	
279	ENTITY EXTRACTION RULES:
280	13. List every distinct entity the claim REFERENCES ‚Äî not just the message-wide entities, but the ones THIS claim depends on.
281	14. The entityType values you may use are exactly: system, customer, licensor, factory, freight_provider, testing_lab, packaging_supplier, service_provider, vendor, person, sku_or_product_line, process_stage, department, geography, document_class.
282	15. \`licensor\` is reserved for entertainment / IP rights holders: Disney, Marvel, Star Wars, Lucasfilm, Warner Bros, NBCUniversal, etc. NEVER use \`vendor\` for those. \`vendor\` is the residual bucket for non-customer non-factory non-licensor business partners.
283	16. \`customer\` is the named retailer / buyer: Burlington, TJX, Ross, Hobby Lobby, Walmart, etc.
284	17. \`factory\` is an overseas manufacturer; \`freight_provider\` / \`testing_lab\` / \`packaging_supplier\` / \`service_provider\` are operational-vendor subtypes ‚Äî pick the specific subtype if it fits, fall back to \`vendor\` only if none does.
285	18. \`system\` is software / tooling: Coldlion, ResourceSpace, Photoshop, Illustrator, Excel, Supabase, Email, WhatsApp, etc.
286	19. \`rawString\` should be how the entity appears in the message text ‚Äî don't normalize to canonical form, the validator does that.
287	20. Omit \`proposedEntities\` entirely if the claim references no proper-noun entities.
288	
289	SENSITIVITY RULES ‚Äî STRICT MODE:
290	21. Set \`sensitivityFlags\` ONLY when the content is clearly sensitive. Operational mentions of named people are NOT sensitive by themselves.
291	22. \`containsSensitiveHRData\` = true ONLY if the message describes: formal disciplinary actions (warnings, PIPs, terminations), compensation specifics (salary numbers, bonus amounts, raise discussions), formal performance reviews/ratings, or formally documented LOA reasons (medical leave, family leave). "Jordan is out next week" is NOT HR data. "We put Jordan on a final-written warning today" IS.
292	23. \`containsSensitivePersonalData\` = true ONLY for explicit personal information: medical conditions or diagnoses, family situations (divorce, deaths, dependents), legal issues, home addresses, personal contact details, or other clearly private personal facts.
293	24. \`isPersonalConflict\` = true ONLY for explicit interpersonal hostility BETWEEN NAMED INDIVIDUALS: shouting matches, harassment, character attacks, or formal complaints. Normal disagreement about a process is NOT a personal conflict.
294	25. If any flag fires, include a brief \`sensitivityReason\` naming which specific text triggered it. Omit \`sensitivityFlags\` entirely when nothing is sensitive ‚Äî that's the common case.
295	26. When in doubt, do NOT set a flag. The cost of a false positive (admin reviews the quarantined candidate manually) is acceptable; the cost of a false negative (sensitive content reaches the knowledge graph) is not. But the threshold is "would a privacy/HR officer at this company call this sensitive?" ‚Äî operational mentions of who works on what don't reach that bar.
296	
297	OUTPUT: Return only the structured JSON matching the schema. No narrative explanation outside the JSON.`;
298	
299	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
300	// Helper: format a conversation segment for the extraction model.
301	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
302	
303	export type FormattedMessage = {
304	  id: string;
305	  role: 'user' | 'assistant' | 'system';
306	  content: string;
307	  authorName: string | null;
308	  createdAt: Date;
309	};
310	
311	export interface FormatConversationSegmentOptions {
312	  carryIn?: FormattedMessage[];
313	}
314	
315	function appendFormattedMessages(lines: string[], msgs: FormattedMessage[]): void {
316	  for (const m of msgs) {
317	    if (m.role === 'system') continue;
318	    const speaker =
319	      m.role === 'assistant' ? '[Oracle - do not extract claims from this]' : `[${m.authorName ?? 'Unknown Employee'}]`;
320	    const timestamp = new Date(m.createdAt).toISOString().slice(0, 16).replace('T', ' ');
321	    lines.push(`Message ID: ${m.id}`);
322	    lines.push(`Speaker: ${speaker}`);
323	    lines.push(`Time: ${timestamp}`);
324	    lines.push(`Content: ${m.content}`);
325	    lines.push('---');
326	  }
327	}
328	
329	/**
330	 * Format a list of messages into a labeled conversation string for the extraction model.
331	 * Each message gets its ID, speaker, timestamp, and content clearly marked.
332	 */
333	export function formatConversationSegment(
334	  msgs: FormattedMessage[],
335	  options: FormatConversationSegmentOptions = {},
336	): string {
337	  const lines: string[] = [];
338	  const carryIn = options.carryIn?.filter((m) => m.role !== 'system') ?? [];
339	  if (carryIn.length > 0) {
340	    lines.push('CONTEXT ONLY - EARLIER MESSAGES:');
341	    lines.push('Use these messages only to interpret the active segment below.');
342	    lines.push('Do NOT extract claims from this context and do NOT quote these message IDs.');
343	    lines.push('---');
344	    appendFormattedMessages(lines, carryIn);
345	  }
346	  lines.push('CONVERSATION SEGMENT TO EXTRACT FROM:');
347	  lines.push('Extract claims only from the messages in this segment. Evidence quotes must use these message IDs only.');
348	  lines.push('---');
349	  appendFormattedMessages(lines, msgs);
350	  return lines.join('\n');
351	}

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * Dynamic model route resolver.
3	 *
4	 * Handles two ID formats:
5	 *   1. Catalog routeId, e.g. "anthropic_claude_haiku_4_5_interview_primary"
6	 *   2. Provider/model id, e.g. "anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5"
7	 *
8	 * This resolver is synchronous and cannot read model_capabilities. Production
9	 * settings should use the DB-aware candidate resolver, which passes verified
10	 * capability flags into synthetic routes and enforces slot requirements.
11	 */
12	
13	import type {
14	  GeminiThinkingStyle,
15	  OracleModelRole,
16	  OracleModelRoute,
17	  OracleProvider,
18	  ReasoningEffort,
19	} from './types';
20	import { ORACLE_MODEL_ROUTES, getOracleRoute } from './catalog';
21	
22	/**
23	 * Derive how a Gemini-family model expresses thinking control, from its model id
24	 * generation. This is a model-generation FACT, derived here in the catalog/
25	 * resolve layer (alongside cacheStrategy/structuredOutputStrategy) so the
26	 * adapter stays free of hard-coded model names.
27	 *
28	 *   Gemini 3.x and newer ‚Üí 'thinking_level' (thinkingConfig.thinkingLevel enum).
29	 *   Gemini 2.x           ‚Üí 'thinking_budget' (thinkingConfig.thinkingBudget int);
30	 *                          2.x REJECTS the thinkingLevel enum with a 400.
31	 *   Non-Gemini / unknown ‚Üí undefined (callers gate on supportsReasoningControls).
32	 *
33	 * Returns undefined for non-google/vertex providers so the field is only set
34	 * where it is meaningful.
35	 */
36	export function geminiThinkingStyleFor(
37	  provider: OracleProvider,
38	  modelId: string,
39	): GeminiThinkingStyle | undefined {
40	  if (provider !== 'google' && provider !== 'vertex') return undefined;
41	  // Extract the leading "gemini-<major>" generation number.
42	  const match = /gemini-(\d+)\b/i.exec(modelId);
43	  if (!match) return 'thinking_budget'; // safest default for unrecognized Gemini ids
44	  const major = Number(match[1]);
45	  return major >= 3 ? 'thinking_level' : 'thinking_budget';
46	}
47	
48	const OR_PROVIDER_MAP: Record<string, OracleProvider> = {
49	  anthropic: 'anthropic',
50	  openai: 'openai',
51	  google: 'google',
52	  deepseek: 'deepseek',
53	  qwen: 'qwen',
54	};
55	
56	function mapPrefix(prefix: string): OracleProvider | null {
57	  return (OR_PROVIDER_MAP[prefix] ?? null) as OracleProvider | null;
58	}
59	
60	const SYNTHETIC_CAPS: Pick<
61	  OracleModelRoute,
62	  | 'supportsVision'
63	  | 'supportsStreaming'
64	  | 'supportsToolCalling'
65	  | 'supportsStructuredOutput'
66	  | 'supportsReasoningControls'
67	  | 'costTier'
68	  | 'enabled'
69	> = {
70	  supportsVision: true,
71	  supportsStreaming: true,
72	  supportsToolCalling: true,
73	  supportsStructuredOutput: true,
74	  supportsReasoningControls: false,
75	  costTier: 'balanced_default',
76	  enabled: true,
77	};
78	
79	export type SyntheticRouteCaps = Partial<typeof SYNTHETIC_CAPS>;
80	
81	function makeSyntheticRoute(
82	  providerModelId: string,
83	  provider: OracleProvider,
84	  modelId: string,
85	  role: OracleModelRole,
86	  caps?: SyntheticRouteCaps,
87	): OracleModelRoute {
88	  const cacheStrategy =
89	    provider === 'anthropic'
90	      ? 'anthropic_auto_plus_explicit'
91	      : provider === 'vertex'
92	        ? 'vertex_implicit_or_explicit_by_context_size'
93	        : provider === 'google'
94	          ? 'none'
95	          : provider === 'deepseek'
96	            ? 'deepseek_automatic_prefix'
97	            : provider === 'qwen'
98	              ? 'qwen_explicit_context_cache'
99	              : 'openai_automatic_with_cache_key';
100	
101	  const structuredOutputStrategy =
102	    provider === 'vertex' || provider === 'google'
103	      ? 'native_json_schema'
104	      : provider === 'openai'
105	        ? 'native_json_schema'
106	        : 'tool_call';
107	
108	  if (!caps) {
109	    console.warn(
110	      `[resolve] using synthetic route for "${providerModelId}" (role=${role}) without DB-verified capabilities. ` +
111	        `Production settings should use resolveRouteCandidates().`,
112	    );
113	  }
114	
115	  return {
116	    routeId: providerModelId,
117	    role,
118	    tier: 'primary',
119	    internalPurpose: null,
120	    provider,
121	    modelId,
122	    displayName: providerModelId,
123	    recommendedUse: `Dynamically resolved from model pool (${providerModelId}).`,
124	    cacheStrategy,
125	    structuredOutputStrategy,
126	    geminiThinkingStyle: geminiThinkingStyleFor(provider, modelId),
127	    ...SYNTHETIC_CAPS,
128	    ...(caps ?? {}),
129	  };
130	}
131	
132	export function providerModelIdForRoute(route: OracleModelRoute): string {
133	  return `${route.provider}/${route.modelId}`;
134	}
135	
136	export function resolveModelRoute(
137	  modelIdOrRouteId: string,
138	  role: OracleModelRole,
139	  reasoningEffort?: ReasoningEffort,
140	  caps?: SyntheticRouteCaps,
141	): OracleModelRoute | null {
142	  let base: OracleModelRoute | null = null;
143	
144	  const byRouteId = getOracleRoute(modelIdOrRouteId);
145	  if (byRouteId) {
146	    base = byRouteId;
147	  } else {
148	    const slashIdx = modelIdOrRouteId.indexOf('/');
149	    if (slashIdx === -1) return null;
150	
151	    const prefix = modelIdOrRouteId.slice(0, slashIdx);
152	    const modelId = modelIdOrRouteId.slice(slashIdx + 1);
153	    const provider = mapPrefix(prefix);
154	    if (!provider) return null;
155	
156	    const catalogMatch = Object.values(ORACLE_MODEL_ROUTES).find(
157	      (r) => r.provider === provider && r.modelId === modelId && r.role === role,
158	    );
159	    base = catalogMatch ?? makeSyntheticRoute(modelIdOrRouteId, provider, modelId, role, caps);
160	  }
161	
162	  // Stamp the Gemini thinking-control style if this is a Gemini-family route and
163	  // a curated catalog entry didn't already pin it. Model-generation fact, so it
164	  // belongs here in the resolve layer, never in the adapter.
165	  if (base.geminiThinkingStyle === undefined) {
166	    const style = geminiThinkingStyleFor(base.provider, base.modelId);
167	    if (style) base = { ...base, geminiThinkingStyle: style };
168	  }
169	
170	  return reasoningEffort ? { ...base, reasoningEffort } : base;
171	}
172
